findings.title: Structural bridge: `/tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/ripgrep/crates/core/flags/defs.rs::impl<Flag for JSON>::update:3504` separates components of [1956, 39]
findings.description: `/tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/ripgrep/crates/core/flags/defs.rs::impl<Flag for JSON>::update:3504` is a structural bridge. Removing it would split the graph into components of sizes [1956, 39]. All communication between these components currently flows through this single node.
findings.suggested_fix: Reduce coupling through this node by introducing alternative call paths, extracting shared interfaces, or splitting the bridge function into independently accessible units.
findings.why_it_matters: A structural bridge is a single node whose failure disconnects entire subsystems. This creates fragile architecture where a bug, API change, or refactor in one function can cascade to all dependent components.

findings.title: unsafe block without SAFETY comment
findings.description: Unsafe blocks should document why they're safe. Add a `// SAFETY:` comment.
findings.why_it_matters: Unsafe code bypasses Rust's safety guarantees. SAFETY comments are essential for code review.

findings.title: Mutual recursion: 3 functions in call cycle (complexity 52)
findings.description: Mutual recursion detected: 3 functions form a call cycle. Cycle: /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/ripgrep/crates/core/main.rs::files_parallel:271 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/ripgrep/crates/core/main.rs::run:77 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/ripgrep/crates/core/main.rs::search_parallel:160. Aggregate complexity: 52.
findings.suggested_fix: Break the cycle by extracting shared logic into a common helper, introducing an event system, or restructuring the call chain to be unidirectional.
findings.why_it_matters: Mutual recursion creates tight coupling between functions, making them impossible to understand, test, or refactor independently. It can also cause stack overflows if the recursion depth is unbounded.

findings.title: Potential infinite loop
findings.description:
Loop with no apparent exit condition detected.

**Analysis:**
📞 Calls: path, to_path_buf, to_path_buf
⚠️ No break/return found in loop body
findings.why_it_matters: Infinite loops without exit conditions will hang the program and consume 100% CPU. Even intentional infinite loops (servers) should have shutdown mechanisms.

findings.title: High panic density in `generate_long`: 6 panic-family calls
findings.description: [COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] Function `generate_long` contains 6 calls to `.unwrap()`, `.expect()`, or `panic!()` at line 35, line 36, line 37, line 39, line 42, line 45. This makes the function fragile and likely to panic at runtime.
findings.suggested_fix:
Reduce panic points by:
- Using `?` operator for error propagation
- Using `unwrap_or` / `unwrap_or_default` / `unwrap_or_else`
- Returning `Result<T, E>` instead of panicking
- Extracting fallible logic into a helper that returns Result
findings.why_it_matters: Functions with many panic points are fragile. A single unexpected None/Err will crash the program. Consolidating error handling makes code more robust and easier to reason about.

findings.title: Mutex poisoning risk
findings.description: [COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] Using .lock().unwrap() will panic if the mutex is poisoned. Consider handling the PoisonError or using parking_lot::Mutex.
findings.suggested_fix:
Handle poisoning: `mutex.lock().unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner())`
Or use `parking_lot::Mutex` which has no poisoning.
findings.why_it_matters: Poisoned mutexes cause cascading panics across threads.

findings.title: Potential ReDoS vulnerability
findings.description:
Regex with nested quantifiers may cause catastrophic backtracking, leading to denial of service.

**Analysis:**
🔍 Pattern issue: nested quantifiers detected
findings.why_it_matters:
ReDoS attacks exploit regex backtracking to consume CPU:
• A malicious input can take exponential time to evaluate
• Single requests can freeze the server
• Input like 'aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa!' on /(a+)+$/ can hang

📝 Pattern: `**a", r"^`
findings.why_it_matters:
ReDoS attacks exploit regex backtracking to consume CPU:
• A malicious input can take exponential time to evaluate
• Single requests can freeze the server
• Input like 'aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa!' on /(a+)+$/ can hang

findings.title: Potential ReDoS vulnerability
findings.description:
Regex with nested quantifiers may cause catastrophic backtracking, leading to denial of service.

**Analysis:**
🔍 Pattern issue: nested quantifiers detected
📝 Pattern: `a**b", r"^`
findings.why_it_matters:
ReDoS attacks exploit regex backtracking to consume CPU:
• A malicious input can take exponential time to evaluate
• Single requests can freeze the server
• Input like 'aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa!' on /(a+)+$/ can hang

findings.title: High panic density in `main`: 9 panic-family calls
findings.description: Function `main` contains 9 calls to `.unwrap()`, `.expect()`, or `panic!()` at line 6, line 11, line 14, line 21, line 22, line 33, line 40, line 45, line 49. This makes the function fragile and likely to panic at runtime.
findings.suggested_fix:
Reduce panic points by:
- Using `?` operator for error propagation
- Using `unwrap_or` / `unwrap_or_default` / `unwrap_or_else`
- Returning `Result<T, E>` instead of panicking
- Extracting fallible logic into a helper that returns Result
findings.why_it_matters: Functions with many panic points are fragile. A single unexpected None/Err will crash the program. Consolidating error handling makes code more robust and easier to reason about.

findings.title: Structural bridge: `/tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/ripgrep/crates/printer/src/color.rs::impl<ColorSpecs>::highlight:258` separates components of [1985, 10]
findings.description: `/tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/ripgrep/crates/printer/src/color.rs::impl<ColorSpecs>::highlight:258` is a structural bridge. Removing it would split the graph into components of sizes [1985, 10]. All communication between these components currently flows through this single node.
findings.suggested_fix: Reduce coupling through this node by introducing alternative call paths, extracting shared interfaces, or splitting the bridge function into independently accessible units.
findings.why_it_matters: A structural bridge is a single node whose failure disconnects entire subsystems. This creates fragile architecture where a bug, API change, or refactor in one function can cascade to all dependent components.

findings.title: Long method: from_low_args (209 lines)
findings.description:
Function 'from_low_args' has 209 lines (effective threshold: 150).

**Graph Analysis:**
Calls 3 different modules - possible split points
findings.suggested_fix:
This function calls 3 different modules. Consider extracting:
  - `handle_flags()` for flags operations
  - `handle_core()` for core operations
  - `handle_src()` for src operations
findings.why_it_matters: Long methods are hard to understand, test, and maintain. Each function should do one thing well.

findings.title: Broad exception catch
findings.description: Catching generic Exception hides bugs and makes debugging difficult.
findings.suggested_fix: Catch specific exceptions instead of generic Exception.
findings.why_it_matters: Broad exception catches mask unexpected errors like TypeErrors or AttributeErrors that indicate bugs in your code.

findings.title: High file-level panic density: 12 panic-family calls
findings.description: File `crates/searcher/src/testutil.rs` contains 12 total panic-family calls (`.unwrap()`, `.expect()`, `panic!()`) outside of test code. Consider refactoring to reduce panic surface.
findings.suggested_fix:
Consider:
- Introducing a module-level error type
- Replacing unwrap chains with `?` propagation
- Using `anyhow::Context` for better error messages
findings.why_it_matters: Files with many panic points are hard to maintain and make the overall application fragile. Centralizing error handling improves reliability.

findings.title: Long method: configs (161 lines)
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] Function 'configs' has 161 lines (effective threshold: 150).

**Graph Analysis:**
Orchestrator pattern: calls 12 functions (reduced severity)
findings.suggested_fix:
This appears to be an orchestrator function (coordinates many calls).
If it must remain long, ensure it:
1. Has clear section comments
2. Handles errors at each step
3. Has a clear flow (consider a state machine for complex flows)
findings.why_it_matters: Long methods are hard to understand, test, and maintain. Each function should do one thing well.
